metadata.summary: Object creation patterns (Factory, Builder, DI, Object Pool) that decouple creation from usage for testable, maintainable code.

content.title: Creational Design Patterns
content.summary: Object creation mechanisms that increase flexibility and reuse of existing code. Factory Method for runtime type selection, Builder for complex multi-step construction, Dependency Injection for loose coupling, Object Pool for expensive resource reuse, Prototype for cloning, Abstract Factory for product families, Singleton for process-global resources (use sparingly).

why.p: Choosing the right pattern directly affects testability and maintainability. Dependency Injection reduces maintenance bugs by 27% while Singleton makes testing 40% more complex. Each pattern solves a specific creation problem at different complexity levels.

list.item: Writing object-creation code where the type is decided at runtime or constructor has many optional params.
Refactoring code with duplicated instantiation chains or sprawling constructors.
Reviewing legacy code for Singleton overuse and proposing DI migrations.
Writing test fixtures and mocks — DI and Factory Method dramatically reduce test scaffolding.
Trivial constructors — `dataclass` / record / struct is enough.
Cross-cutting infra concerns where DI containers already exist (FastAPI, NestJS, Spring).
One-off scripts where pattern overhead exceeds the value.
High-performance hot paths where DI lookup cost matters.

rule.statement: Default to Dependency Injection for any component with dependencies. Never reach for Singleton unless the dependency is truly process-global and read-only.
rule.rationale: DI reduces maintenance bugs by 27%, improves testability (70% of high-performing teams use mocking), enables modularity, and accelerates refactoring. DI frameworks exist in every language.
